title: Everything You Need to Know About the UC Schools
body: I am a high school dropout with a cumulative GPA below a 1.0. After getting my GED, I spent a few years in CC and graduated with a 3.9 GPA, earned multiple AA’s and got accepted into Cal, UC Irvine and UC Davis. Community College gives everyone the opportunity to reinvent themselves, to mature, and to find direction in life. I did not have a goal of transferring to a four-year university when I entered CC but destiny had other plans. I am now currently finishing my first year at Cal and have loved every minute of it. Please do not fall into the stigma of chasing a “dream school”. No matter which university you are led to, know that, that is where you were called to go and everything will work out!

title: Everything You Need to Know About the UC Schools
body:
to any high school seniors bummed out about their decision results: dont be afraid of community colleges! there’s a stigma against CCs.. but if I could go back in time to choose between university or CC, I’d choose community all over again. i hated it at first but only because i pressured myself into feeling like i should have gone to a CSU or UC. there’s no wrong path! it doesn’t matter how long you take.

CC’s helped me save money, the classes were smaller and I could be more personal with the professors. finally it was much easier to get into the universities I wanted due to the transfer system! CC transfers are given priority over high school graduates. Out of high school I pretty much got waitlisted and rejected from every place I applied even though I took AP’s, had great grades and extracurriculars. This year as a transfer applicant, I got into all the schools I got rejected from and even into schools I thought I wasn’t good enough for!

whatever you choose, its okay that its not the plan you had! be kind to yourself, i know i wasn’t to myself back then :)

title: Everything You Need to Know About the UC Schools
body:
Love seeing UCSB move up the ranks. As an alum, moving up the career ladder takes a lot of soft skills (presentations, listening, speaking with management). You really have to learn to be a people person if you want to move to management and so forth.

If you do your part in socializing (talking to people, partying, connecting with people) this will really help your career. So don't just think it's all about technical skills and the major. The great thing about UCSB is that you are only surrounded by other college students, so it forces you to be in that position all the time. But don't party too much. Still put in the work to learn your academics.

Technical Skills + People skills will be able to help you advance in your career.

title: Everything You Need to Know About the UC Schools
body:
I went to UCSD thinking "ehh it's not Berkeley or UCLA". Do not go in thinking that. UCSD kids are smart as hell and they put the work in. Geisel Library has a 24 hour section and I've been there at 4-5 am and seen it still packed with people. UCSD is a great education but they will make you work for it.

EDIT: I'll also add it wasn't listed as a con but the campus is way too overcrowded. I think all UCs are really like this, but I watched UCSD go from 33k to 40k undergraduates in just five years, and we had some new buildings come up but not nearly at the same pace. The dining halls became so crowded at lunch time that I would either get food elsewhere or eat lunch at a different time.

title: UC admissions REACTS to TikTok application advice
body:
This felt very down-to-earth and honest. Although I've graduated from CSU, this was a very positive and refreshing take from the UC admissions team. It's also really great that UC dispelled this idea that there is an exact formula, now are there cases of people being accepted for similar trope i.e., extracurriculars, community service, academic achievement, etc..... yeah sure, and I think people have this idea of a formula in place because people have these common denominators, but in reality and in a more personal level these common denominators are something deeper than what people see at surface level, everyone's experience is unique and different.

I think the big question is to discern whether your experience is "competitive" (I use this term lightly), by gauging your intent. You should be asking yourself, "Am I doing all these activities and stuff because I like them, or am I doing them just so I can get into the best school?" Typically, the people who love the activities don't usually have this question in mind (though there are exceptions). Anyone can say they volunteered for a puppy shelter, and actually be the person who sits on their phone and does not contribute.

If you're in either of these groups, don't stress too much; you are figuring things out at the end of the day and will continue to do so for the rest of your life, like everyone else on this planet. If you make it into your dream college, congratulations! If you don't, please don't fret because there are so many more opportunities and experiences out there that are waiting to be discovered by you than some old university.

Great video, University of California!

title: Everything You Need to Know About UC Davis
body: I had a blast at UC Davis, Davis really felt like a college town. I am so glad I went here over UC Berkeley. The tip about studying abroad is one that I highly recommend as well. I did the UCEAP program to Korea and Japan. I did it twice because it was a life changing experience. And the tuition for me to go to Korea / Japan was actually cheaper than my tuition at UC Davis. There is something about being in a study abroad program that you cannot recreate by personal travels. Another tip is to apply to be a PAL program, this pairs you up with an international student and you basically hangout together and you get a 0.5 unit credit for doing so. I did the PAL program every quarter to meet the minimum units requirement to be a full-time student.

title: StoryTime | Why I HATED UC Davis
body: I had a friend who went to UC Davis and who majored in Mathematics and now teaches high school mathematics (e.g., he is currently teaching pre-calculus).  He would agree with you that the academics are competitive at UC Davis and the grading was tough but fair (it can be curved especially in Mathematics and Chemistry classes and Physics class [He had to take one Physics class in order to graduate while he needed three chemistry classes or the equivalent {3 Physics classes and 1 Chemistry class}]) and the classwork was challenging (even in classes that are not supposed to be challenging like Freshman Composition and Economics [Introductory]).  For example, when he was a Freshman at UC Davis, he found his first General Chemistry 2A class just as difficult as AP Chemistry semester 1 and General Chemistry 2B Class just as difficult as AP Chemistry semester 2.  In addition, his General Chemistry classes were curved (e.g., you need at least 60% to pass with a C-).   Plus, he started off with Calculus 2 or Math 21B and found it to be a near repeat of AP Calculus BC (it did not have series calculus while Math 21C did).  Like his Chemistry classes, his lower division math classes were also curved by not by much (e.g., A-=88% or higher, B-=76% or higher, C-=64% or higher).   Upper division math classes had better grading curves, but the work was significantly tougher.  He was a B+ average student at UC Davis and did go to SDSU for graduate school in teaching.  He did make a lot of friends at UC Davis and had a mostly good time at UC Davis but despised the weather (it was mostly cold especially in the late fall or winter and early spring) and had trouble getting housing in his second, third and fourth years and had trouble getting into some of the classes he wanted or needed.  UC Davis like college is what you make the most of it.  If anyone wants to attend UC Davis, they got to work very hard and smart and when they get into UC Davis, they got to work even harder and smarter.

body: I transferred from a community college to UCD, and I have to say that I loved it my last three years.  I ended up staying two years beyond graduation while working in Sacramento. As an openly gay Native American-Mexican American  UCD student in the 80`s, I dealt with some homophobia, but overall, I enjoyed my experience in Davis.  I only left because of a career change that required a new location.  It is one of the few places I would consider moving back to from living in Europe. title: StoryTime | Why I HATED UC Davis
body:
Point 1: Every college, even ivies have TAs grade. It’s how college works

Point 2: The student body is 20% white, it was ranked the most diverse college in the nation by QS

Point 3: If you don’t like the smaller town the 5th largest city in CA is only 15minutes away. How do you get there? Well the bus ride is free for UC Davis students.

Point 4: the alternative to bikes is walking. Even on a small campus like UCLA you actually end up spending more time walking than you would biking on a big campus like Davis just because a bike gets you places so much faster. Bike accidents also almost virtually never happen… that’s just a fact. If they do the worst thing that can happen is you get a scraped hand.

Point 5: It only gets above 80 during the summer… when students aren’t there.

Point 6: The cow smell is only by Tercero. If you don’t live there you’re never going to smell it. If you live in Tercero you can submit a transfer request to Segundo or Cuarto which are very far from the cows.

title: Let's talk about "ivy day" (college admissions)
body: not to knock elite universities, i’m sure it’s a great experience and that they give great educations, but i also feel like i should add on as a recent college grad that you can get equally great experiences, education, and connections from just going to a public state school! it’s a fraction of the cost (especially if you’re already in-state), and depending on the program and the school, it gives you experience that can easily compete with elite programs. i majored in journalism, which is hella competitive right now, but i went to a school in my state with a very good program and got a pretty good job that can open a lot of doors just a couple months after graduation. my best friend is the smartest person i’ve ever met, went to a school with a 27% acceptance rate, and is still looking for a job (albeit in a different but equally competitive field) over six months after graduation. so basically if you don’t get into your dream school, there is absolutely no shame in not going elite at all!

body: it can sound so cheesy and not at all what you want to hear in the midst of getting rejected from a dream school, but it is true what people say - there is always a path to what you want to do and it is never too late to redirect, change what you're aiming for, and start again. i was that kid that DID get into my "dream school" and it ended up sending me into a huge spiral and depression slump once i got there. i transferred to a public state school after my first year, changed degree paths and ended up graduating a year early. i'm getting my doctorate and teach undergrads regularly now and it feels so important to share stories of setbacks and how you can overcome them, because rejection from your first choice and other academic pitfalls can feel so isolating.

title: Let's talk about "ivy day" (college admissions)
body:
oddly enough, i've never felt the wealth gap as acutely as i do right now. my parents grew up too poor to dream (and, y'know, depression), so i never learned to aim any higher than hopefully 'making do.' i had the academic record for more prestigious schools — that is, the kind that actually reject applicants — but i'd never seriously considered the possibility of being able to afford tuition, let alone the quintessential on- (or near-) campus living experience.

shoutout to all my fellow community college dropouts former attendees and trade school students who probably won't be able to move out until they're at least 30; to the 40-year-old single moms working on the degrees they never got when they were my age; to the young adults who don't know how to cope with the fact that they're no longer teenagers; to the late-20-somethings who feel like their adulthood still hasn't really started; to all the other young people who, despite always assuming they would, couldn't get a college degree straight out of high school because they worked themselves too hard for too long; to all the neurodivergent and queer people who have been rejected by classmates and students for being too different; to all the americans who don't know how they'll pay for medical care with only a diploma.



if you're anything like me, you can still find hope in the night program at your local cosmetology school. i may be bald but goddammit, hair saved my life <3

title: Let's talk about "ivy day" (college admissions)
body:
This was literally me 2 years ago. In retrospect I couldn’t be happier going into my 4th semester of my non Ivy League college. My dream school was Princeton and I got flat out rejected. I recorded a reaction video for it too. However, with the knowledge I have now and the people I’ve met in my college, if I had gotten accepted into Princeton I would not have thrived nearly as much. I went to a competitive high school where it was common to see people getting accepted into ivy leagues. I felt terrible about myself when I wasn’t accepted, but just because I’m not going to an Ivy League school doesn’t mean I’m not being challenged. I’ve learned so much and since the school I’m in is less competitive my mental health has improved tremendously. I’ve been in competitive programs since I was 9 years old and it took the fun out of learning. Going to my non competitive college I’ve started enjoying learning again, and the price is amazing. I will have no debt for my undergrad, my gpa looks great, and I can still attend as an honors student in the college. The world didn’t end when I wasn’t accepted, in fact it started an amazing chain reaction that I couldn’t see until I attended the college as a student. Funnily enough the school I said I would never attend wound up being the one I’m in now. I wouldn’t trade it for Princeton and I can confidently say I am exactly where I am supposed to be right now. I hope this helps someone, there is so much pressure on you between 16-18 to prepare for college, but even if not getting into your dream school is the worst case scenario, you will still be able to thrive. My biggest fear happened and I am grateful for it. Lots of love ❤

(Side note: it’s 2 a.m. as I’m writing this, so don’t mind the grammar)

body: When I was 17 I visited two colleges in which part of their campus tours were exactly the same. I swear, EXACTLY the same word for word. Penn State and Virginia Tech both told me they had the most friendly mail man in the world called Mike or Dave or something. Apparently, if you ever forgot to send your mom a card on her birthday and showed up at the post office, Mike the mailman would first give you a short lecture about organization and then do you a favor by putting a "lost in mail" stamp on your envelope to make it look like it was sent on time. I can't recite it exactly but I remember both campus literally used the exact same script. The guide even phrased it by saying "I even heard that..." to make it sound like some secret inside scoop you could only get from a student.
